title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to an i.i.d. sequence of tasks. The authors address a crucial problem of uncertainty quantification in meta-learning, particularly relevant for safety-critical applications like healthcare or autonomous vehicles, as highlighted in the introduction. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to quickly adapt a predictor to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state). The chosen datasets, mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and CDC Heart Dataset, span diverse domains, demonstrating the broad applicability of the proposed method.

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. The problem statement in the introduction and Section 4.1 is clear, highlighting the limitations of existing methods that only provide guarantees on average across future tasks. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. This distinction is clearly articulated in Figure 2, where the proposed Meta PAC guarantee (2d) shows a more fine-grained conditional control over the different sources of randomness (calibration tasks, test task adaptation data, and test data) compared to the Meta-CP (2c). Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. The introduction of three sources of randomness (controlled by $\delta$, $\alpha$, and $\epsilon$) and their hierarchical conditioning (Definition 3) is complex. While Figure 2 provides an excellent visual summary, a more intuitive, high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms). This would greatly aid readers in grasping the core contribution before delving into the technical specifics of Section 4.1.

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). The focus on providing guarantees that hold for *most* future tasks and *most* future examples within those tasks directly addresses a critical need for robust uncertainty quantification in real-world, safety-critical meta-learning applications, aligning with the paper's introductory motivation.
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). Algorithm 1, particularly the use of `PS-BINOM` (Algorithm 2 in Appendix B) for individual task calibration and then again for combining these parameters, demonstrates a clever and efficient use of existing PAC methods.
- Empirical results seem strong. The experiments across mini-ImageNet, FewRel, and CDC Heart Dataset provide compelling evidence for the efficacy of Meta-PS. Figures 3a, 4a, and 5a consistently show that the proposed Meta-PS method satisfies the $\epsilon = 0.1$ error constraint within the desired probability levels (e.g., $1-\alpha=0.9$ of the time), unlike PS and Meta-CP which often violate it. Furthermore, Meta-PS achieves this while producing significantly smaller prediction set sizes (Figures 3b, 4b, 5b) compared to the over-conservative PS-Test baseline. The plots in Figure 3c and Figure 6 specifically demonstrate that Meta-PS effectively controls the prediction set error over different test distributions given a fixed calibration dataset, a key advantage over Meta-CP.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy. This fixed threshold, while offering strong guarantees, might lead to suboptimal prediction set sizes for individual tasks, especially those that are "easy" to predict, potentially resulting in unnecessarily large sets. This could be a practical limitation when fine-grained, task-specific uncertainty quantification is paramount.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. For instance, the mini-ImageNet experiments use N=500 calibration tasks, and the CDC Heart Dataset uses N=250. Acquiring such a large number of distinct, yet i.i.d., tasks with sufficient labeled examples for calibration (n=2500 for mini-ImageNet, n=3000 for CDC Heart) can be a significant practical hurdle in many real-world meta-learning applications, limiting the method's applicability. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact. While the combination is novel and useful, the foundational concepts are well-established.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calibration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second? Could the authors clarify if the `p_pi` distribution over tasks ensures that the test task distribution is truly drawn i.i.d. from the same overarching distribution as the calibration tasks, even if the specific classes within the tasks (e.g., 16 classes for calibration, 20 for testing in Mini-ImageNet) are disjoint? The setup for Mini-ImageNet (Section 5.2) describes drawing from `(16 choose 5)` possible tasks for calibration and `(20 choose 5)` for testing. This distinction needs to be reconciled with the i.i.d. assumption for `theta_i` from `p_pi` (Section 4.1).
- Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing. A more detailed explanation of the `g` function defined as `g(τ, y) := τ * (y = 1)` and the role of the "dummy label 1" would be beneficial. It appears to be a clever way to reframe the problem of finding a prediction interval for the estimated $\tau_i$ values into a standard PAC prediction set problem, but this abstraction is not immediately clear.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work in Section 6 and Appendix F. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. Specifically, reiterating that the rigorous PAC guarantees are conditional on assumptions such as the i.i.d. nature of tasks and the availability of sufficient calibration data, would be valuable for users. This would help prevent misuse due to an incomplete understanding of the theoretical underpinnings and their practical implications. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.
reviews.questions: Please refer to weaknesss above.
reviews.limitations: Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
This paper introduces a novel PAC (Probably Approximately Correct) prediction set algorithm specifically designed for the meta-learning setting. The core objective is to enable predictors to quickly adapt to new, unseen tasks using only a limited number of labeled training examples, while providing rigorous uncertainty quantification. The authors address a critical shortcoming of existing meta-learning prediction set approaches, which often lack guarantees that hold with high probability over future tasks or conditionally over adaptation sets. Their proposed method constructs prediction sets that satisfy an extended PAC guarantee, controlling three distinct sources of randomness related to calibration tasks, test adaptation sets, and evaluation examples (controlled by ε, α, and δ respectively, as detailed in Section 4.1 and Definition 3). Through comprehensive experiments on four diverse datasets spanning visual (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), language (FewRel), and medical (CDC Heart Dataset) domains, the paper demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed Meta-PS algorithm. The results consistently show that Meta-PS empirically satisfies the desired PAC guarantee while simultaneously producing prediction sets that are notably smaller in size compared to other baselines that attempt to meet similar guarantees.

**Strengths And Weaknesses:**

**Strengths:**

*   **The paper is well-written.** The manuscript is very clear and well-structured, making it easy to follow the problem setup, proposed algorithm, and theoretical guarantees. The introduction effectively highlights the limitations of prior work, such as meta-conformal prediction, by articulating the need for guarantees that hold with high probability over future tasks and conditionally over adaptation sets (as explained in Figure 2d vs. 2c). The definitions in Section 3 and the problem statement in Section 4.1 are precisely formulated, laying a solid foundation for the theoretical analysis.
*   **The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.** The Meta-PS algorithm (Algorithm 1) presents a sound two-step approach: meta-calibration across multiple tasks followed by the construction of a prediction interval over the derived parameters. This design is intuitively appealing as it leverages the i.i.d. nature of tasks to generalize prediction set parameters. The theoretical underpinnings are robust, with Theorem 1 formally proving that the Meta-PS estimator is (ε, α, δ)-meta probably approximately correct (mPAC) for the task distribution pπ. This theoretical guarantee directly addresses the fine-grained control over various sources of randomness that the authors set out to achieve. The explanation in Section 4.3 provides good intuition for why the approach works, linking the empirical distribution of scalar parameters to the i.i.d. nature of tasks.
*   **Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.** The empirical validation is extensive and thorough, utilizing four distinct datasets across three application domains: mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C (visual), FewRel (language), and CDC Heart Dataset (medical). This broad selection convincingly demonstrates the general applicability and robustness of the Meta-PS approach. The comparison against relevant baselines—a naive PAC prediction set (PS), a test-data-rich PAC prediction set (PS-Test), and the PAC variant of meta conformal prediction (Meta-CP)—provides a clear picture of the proposed method's advantages. For instance, as shown in Figure 3a, Figure 4a, and Figure 5a, Meta-PS consistently meets the target error rate (e.g., ε=0.1) without being overly conservative like PS-Test. Furthermore, Figures 3c and 6 are particularly insightful, empirically demonstrating that Meta-PS successfully controls the prediction set error over the randomness of test adaptation data, a crucial distinction from Meta-CP.

**Weakness:**

*   **For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?** The paper states in Section 4.2 that reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation is valid because it was chosen after adaptation via a hold-out set from the same distribution as the calibration set. However, a key aspect of meta-learning is adaptation to *new* tasks. While the current approach is efficient by not requiring additional labeled examples for calibration on the test distribution, exploring whether the prediction set parameter itself could be fine-tuned or adapted specifically to the characteristics of the *new* test task's adaptation set might yield even tighter or more precise prediction sets for that particular task. This could potentially offer a trade-off between label efficiency and ultimate prediction set quality.
*   **The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).** While Algorithm 1's description mentions using "any meta learning and adaptation algorithm" (Line 3), the experimental section (Section 5.1, "Score functions") explicitly states that a prototypical network is used as the score function. Prototypical networks are a specific type of metric-based meta-learning. Extending the empirical validation to include optimization-based meta-learning approaches like MAML (Finn et al., 2017) would significantly broaden the demonstrated applicability of the proposed Meta-PS framework. This would reassure readers that the method is indeed generalizable across different meta-learning paradigms, not just a specific subset.
*   **For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.** In Section 5.2, the mini-ImageNet setup is described as "5-shot 5-way learning," which is a common benchmark. However, evaluating the method on more challenging classification tasks, such as 10-way or 20-way classification, would provide a stronger demonstration of its robustness and scalability. Increasing the number of classes per task often leads to greater ambiguity and harder decision boundaries, potentially revealing more about the method's performance under increased complexity and the ability of the prediction sets to maintain coverage and size.

**Questions:**
Please refer to the weaknesses above for specific questions regarding adaptation of prediction set parameters and broader applicability to different meta-learning algorithms and more challenging experimental setups.

**Limitations:**
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations (e.g., requirement for enough calibration datapoints) and potential negative societal impact of their work (e.g., misuse of guarantees without proper understanding of assumptions) in Section 6.

**Rating:**
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. The authors identify a critical gap in existing uncertainty quantification methods for meta-learning, particularly for safety-critical systems where guarantees must hold with high probability over future tasks. Since there are multiple distributions involved in the meta-learning setting—namely, training, calibration, and test task distributions—the considered PAC notion for meta learning is carefully distinguished from existing notions designed for conventional machine learning settings, such as those discussed in [3], which typically assume i.i.d. data. Furthermore, the meta-learning setting is also different from the covariate shift case considered in [6], as adaptation datasets are available for a trained model to adapt to these associated distributions without requiring the explicit estimation of importance weights. This distinction is crucial for the practical applicability of the proposed method.

To address these unique challenges, a novel meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3). This definition meticulously accounts for three sources of randomness: an adaptation set and calibration set for each calibration task, an adaptation set for a new task, and an evaluation set for the same new task. Specifically, it uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, \ldots, p_{\theta_N}$, to generate an $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ with high probability $1-\delta$. This three-parameter PAC-style guarantee (", ↵, ) is illustrated clearly in Figure 2d, highlighting its fine-grained control over correctness conditioning compared to prior work. Algorithm 1, named Meta-PS, is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of the prediction set. This algorithm employs a two-step strategy: it first determines ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimators/thresholds ($\tau_i$) on each of the N calibration distributions (Line 3, using Algorithm 2 in Appendix B for `PS-BINOM`). These individual thresholds, each assigned a dummy label '1', then form a new labeled sample (S0). In the second step, these new samples are used to construct a prediction interval for $\tau$, effectively determining a final threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC (Line 4). Theorem 1 formally shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC, demonstrating its theoretical soundness.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in the meta-learning case, where multiple distributions are present and an augmented calibration set is available. The authors clearly articulate how their proposed meta-PAC property, illustrated in Figure 2d, offers a more robust and fine-grained control over correctness guarantees compared to existing approaches like meta conformal prediction [14] (Figure 2c). Specifically, the proposed approach ensures that the correctness guarantee holds conditionally over adaptation sets for a new task, which is a critical improvement for practical meta-learning scenarios in safety-critical applications. The algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction, leveraging established techniques in a novel hierarchical manner. This extension allows for effective uncertainty quantification when rapidly adapting models to new, unseen tasks using only a few examples, without needing additional calibration data for the target task itself.

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear. The problem setup in Section 4.1 is precisely defined, laying a solid foundation for the subsequent theoretical developments. Theorem 1, which guarantees the (", ↵, )-mPAC property for Algorithm 1, is a central contribution, and its complete proof in Appendix C provides rigorous backing for the proposed method. The experimental validation further reinforces the quality of the work, demonstrating the efficacy of Meta-PS across diverse application domains including visual (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), language (FewRel), and medical (CDC Heart Dataset). Figures 3a, 4a, and 5a consistently show that Meta-PS empirically satisfies the desired error constraint (e.g., " = 0.1) at the specified probability levels, outperforming or matching baselines. Moreover, Figures 3b, 4b, and 5b highlight the practical advantage of Meta-PS in producing smaller prediction set sizes compared to conservative alternatives like PS-Test, while still meeting the rigorous PAC guarantees.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow. The introduction effectively motivates the problem by highlighting the need for robust uncertainty quantification in safety-critical meta-learning. The graphical representation in Figure 2 is particularly helpful for understanding the nuanced differences between various types of prediction set guarantees. The step-by-step description of Algorithm 1, along with the intuitive explanation of its two-step calibration process in Section 4.2 and Section 4.3, makes the approach very comprehensible. The experimental setup and results are presented clearly, with box plots effectively illustrating the empirical error and size distributions.

Significance:
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting, offering a significant advancement for the deployment of machine learning models in high-stakes environments. The ability to construct PAC prediction sets that adapt to new tasks with rigorous, task-specific guarantees, as demonstrated by Meta-PS, is highly relevant for fields such as autonomous driving and healthcare. The method's capacity to maintain small prediction set sizes while satisfying these guarantees makes it a practical and efficient solution for real-world applications where both safety and utility are paramount.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 (PS-BINOM) involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search? Given that Algorithm 1 calls PS-BINOM `N` times (Line 3) and then once more (Line 4), understanding the practical implications of this grid search in terms of computational overhead would be valuable for wider adoption, especially with large `N` or very fine granularities.

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. Section 6 explicitly mentions that the approach requires "enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee," referencing [3] for further analysis. Furthermore, the discussion on potential societal impact in Section 6 and Appendix F demonstrates a responsible consideration of the broader implications of their research, including the risk of misuse if guarantees are not fully understood.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct. The theoretical underpinnings, particularly Theorem 1 and its proof in Appendix C, appear sound. The paper correctly surveys the landscape of conformal prediction and meta-learning, positioning its contribution within existing literature by highlighting the distinctions from prior works like [14].

I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. This is a notable contribution, as it extends the concept of PAC guarantees to a more granular level within the meta-learning framework. Specifically, the proposed (", ↵,  )-meta probably approximately correct (mPAC) guarantee, as formalized in Definition 3 and visually represented in Figure 2d, explicitly controls three distinct sources of randomness: the test example ("), the test task and its adaptation data (↵), and the calibration tasks along with their adaptation and calibration data ( ). While this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results by chaining concentration arguments, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before in this precise form. This multi-layered conditional guarantee offers a more stringent form of uncertainty quantification, which could be particularly relevant for highly sensitive applications.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. [14] (Meta-CP) in that it also conditions on the tasks. As illustrated in Figure 2, the key distinction lies in the conditioning: Figure 2c (Meta-CP) shows a guarantee conditioned on the entire test task, meaning the correctness does not necessarily hold conditionally over adaptation sets. In contrast, the proposed Meta-PS (Figure 2d) provides a correctness guarantee conditioned on the *adapted* prediction set, meaning it holds for most future datasets within the same task after adaptation. This finer-grained control over the correctness guarantee with respect to the adaptation set is a crucial difference and may prove useful for practitioners requiring robust, task-specific guarantees.

This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters. This hierarchical structure, controlled by the parameters ", ↵, and  as defined in Definition 3, aims to provide strong statistical guarantees across different levels of randomness inherent in meta-learning. The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration, as evidenced by Algorithm 1 which internally utilizes PS-BINOM (Algorithm 2) twice. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine, covering diverse datasets such as mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and the CDC Heart Dataset.

**Strengths And Weaknesses:**
**Strengths:** I the technical results are clearly correct. The formal definition of the mPAC guarantee (Definition 3) and the proof of Theorem 1 in Appendix C provide a solid theoretical foundation for the proposed algorithm. The step-by-step construction of the Meta-PS algorithm (Algorithm 1), leveraging existing PAC prediction set algorithms, is well-articulated and logically sound. The experiments are comprehensive. The evaluation spans four distinct datasets across three application domains (visual, language, medical), demonstrating the method's applicability and robustness. The comparison against relevant baselines (PS, PS-Test, Meta-CP) on metrics like prediction set error and size, as shown in Figures 3, 4, and 5, empirically validates the theoretical claims, particularly the ability of Meta-PS to satisfy the specified error constraint (") while maintaining smaller set sizes compared to conservative alternatives like PS-Test. The detailed analysis, including Figure 3c and 6, which disaggregate error distributions given fixed calibration sets, further strengthens the empirical evidence for the mPAC property.

**Weaknesses:**

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used. It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters. The introduction of three distinct probability parameters (", ↵,  ) to control different layers of randomness, while theoretically rigorous, makes practical application and communication of the guarantee challenging. A user would need to carefully consider how to choose appropriate values for ", ↵, and  based on their specific application's risk tolerance, which might be a non-trivial task for many machine learning practitioners unfamiliar with such multi-layered statistical guarantees. This complexity could hinder adoption despite the theoretical strength.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. While the caption attempts to explain elements like stripe patterns and the 'N' box, the overall visual density and abstract nature of the graphical models make it hard for readers not deeply immersed in this specific notation to quickly grasp the distinctions between the different guarantees. The orientations of the stripes, for example, are mentioned as indicating conditioned variables, but the specific visual encoding might not be immediately intuitive. What does the box with “N” mean? Although explained as "N copies of the variables," its visual representation adds to the complexity. Another part that is hard to read is 4.1. This section, "Setup and Problem," introduces a multitude of random variables and nested probabilities (e.g., p✓0i, S, A, p⇡, A, S0, Definition 3) in a very compact manner. The rapid introduction of complex notation and the intricate description of the three sources of randomness and their conditioning make it challenging for a general machine learning audience to follow without significant effort and prior expertise in PAC-style guarantees. There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel. The meta conformal prediction paper [14] already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. While the core algorithmic technique of chaining concentration arguments is a known statistical approach, its application to achieve this specific, finely-grained conditional guarantee in the meta-learning setting is indeed novel. The authors correctly point out in Section 2 that existing meta conformal prediction [14] "does not control error over the randomness in the adaptation test examples," which is precisely what their meta-PAC property (Figure 2d) aims to address. Thus, for safety-critical systems where guarantees must hold for *each* adapted task instance, this extension provides a crucial additional layer of assurance. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm.

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that. Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. While Park et al. [3] successfully applied Vovk's ideas in a deep learning context, the fundamental theoretical concept of constructing prediction sets with PAC guarantees, conditional on the training data, originates from Vovk's earlier work. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm (Algorithm 2 in Appendix B), which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21]. The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

**Questions:**
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? The assumption of tasks being i.i.d. draws from a meta-distribution p⇡ (as described in Section 3.2 and 4.1) is a strong theoretical idealization. In practical safety-critical scenarios, such as autonomous driving where "future tasks might be new regions" or healthcare where "future tasks might be new patient cohort" (Introduction), it is often challenging to guarantee that these new tasks are truly i.i.d. from the distribution of calibration tasks. Real-world tasks might exhibit temporal dependencies, covariate shifts, or other forms of non-stationarity that violate the i.i.d. assumption, potentially impacting the practical validity of the derived guarantees. What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold? Despite the strong i.i.d. assumption, the paper's focus on safety-critical systems suggests scenarios where even an idealized, strong guarantee is preferred over none. For instance, in highly controlled environments or when tasks are carefully curated to ensure minimal distributional shift, the guarantee could be more directly applicable. The CDC Heart Dataset, for example, uses data from different years and US states, which might be considered "tasks," but their i.i.d. nature needs careful justification in a real-world deployment context.

**Limitations:**
I believe the limitations have been addressed. The paper explicitly discusses the requirement for "enough calibration datapoints to satisfy the PAC guarantee" and acknowledges the "potential societal impact... that a user of the proposed algorithm might misuse the guarantee without a proper understanding of assumptions" in Section 6. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

**Rating:**
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.
reviews.questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.
reviews.limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

**Summary:**
The paper introduces a novel approach for constructing PAC prediction sets in the meta-learning paradigm, specifically designed to adapt a predictor to new tasks with minimal new examples. The core contribution lies in estimating prediction sets during the meta-test stage, leveraging parameters learned from meta-train adaptation and calibration phases, thereby circumventing the need for extensive calibration data during deployment on a new task. This entire framework is meticulously engineered to adhere to the Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) setting, offering robust theoretical guarantees for generating these meta-test prediction sets without requiring additional calibration on the target task itself. The efficacy of the proposed method, Meta-PS, is empirically validated across a diverse set of four datasets spanning three distinct application domains: mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C in the visual domain, FewRel in the language domain, and the CDC Heart Dataset in the medical domain, demonstrating competitive results against existing baselines.

**Strengths And Weaknesses:**
**Strengths:**
1.  A primary strength of this work is that the proposed Meta-PS method is underpinned by a rigorous PAC bound, specifically a novel meta-PAC guarantee (Definition 3). This theoretical backing ensures that the prediction sets generated will contain the true label with high probability across future tasks, a crucial property for safety-critical applications. Unlike existing approaches that offer guarantees only on average across tasks or without control over adaptation set randomness, the authors clearly delineate how their meta-PAC property provides a more fine-grained control over various sources of randomness, as illustrated effectively in Figure 2d.

**Weaknesses:**
1.  The overall presentation of the paper could be significantly improved for clarity. The notation, in particular, is very confusing and at times misleading, which impedes understanding of the proposed algorithm and its theoretical underpinnings. For instance, the distinction between `F_tau(x)`, `F_tau(A, x)`, and `F_tau(A, x, y)` as prediction sets or score functions, or the various forms of `\tau` and `\hat{\tau}`, is not always immediately clear, requiring careful re-reading of Section 3 and 4. This lack of notational consistency across different contexts makes it challenging to follow the mathematical formulation.
2.  A broader conceptual overview or 'big picture' is notably absent in key introductory sections and figures. For example, while the text mentions `f` as a 'meta-learned and potentially adaptable score function' in Section 1 and 'meta-learned score function' in Section 4, this critical context is missing in Figure 1. Figure 1, which aims to illustrate the learning process, would greatly benefit from explicitly labeling `f` as a pre-trained meta-model or indicating its origin, as its current depiction might confuse readers familiar with meta-learning workflows, who expect to see the initial meta-learning phase clearly represented before adaptation.

**Questions:**
1.  Regarding the empirical evaluation, both prediction set error and prediction set size are indeed critical metrics. The results, particularly for mini-ImageNet (Figure 3a and 3b) and FewRel (Figure 4a and 4b), show that Meta-PS achieves the desired low set error, typically below the specified `\epsilon = 0.1` threshold, while its set size is often larger than Meta-CP but smaller than PS-Test. While the ideal scenario is to achieve both minimal error and minimal size, the paper's primary claim is about satisfying the PAC guarantee. Could the authors provide a more explicit discussion and a clearer conclusion regarding the trade-off observed between prediction set error and size when comparing Meta-CP and Meta-PS? Specifically, given that Meta-CP often has a smaller set size but fails to meet the `\epsilon` error criterion as consistently as Meta-PS (e.g., Figure 3c), a discussion on why the proposed meta-PAC guarantee (Figure 2d) is a more desirable property, even if it sometimes leads to larger sets, would be beneficial. It seems Meta-PS prioritizes the robustness of the guarantee over minimal size, but this could be articulated more directly.
2.  Algorithm 1, Meta-PS, explicitly states that 'any PAC prediction set algorithms are used to implement an estimator `\hat{\epsilon}, \delta`' and that PS-BINOM (Algorithm 2) is used in Appendix B. Given that the choice of estimator for `\tau` is a modular component, do different underlying PAC prediction set estimators (beyond PS-BINOM) significantly impact the overall performance of Meta-PS, both in terms of satisfying the meta-PAC guarantee and achieving a small prediction set size? A brief discussion on the sensitivity to this choice, or the theoretical implications of using alternative estimators, would add valuable insight.
3.  There appears to be an inconsistency or at least a lack of clarity regarding the input spaces of the score functions mentioned in Section 3.2, particularly around lines 106-108. Line 106 describes `f: X x Y -> R` for meta-learning *without* adaptation, while line 108 defines `f_A(x,y) := f(A, x, y)` where `f: Z x X x Y -> R`. Can the authors clarify this distinction? Is `f` in line 106 a different function than the `f` used to define `f_A` in line 108? My understanding aligns with the general meta-learning context, where `f` (or a base model) is typically an off-the-shelf meta-learned model that is then adapted via an adaptation set `A` to become `f_A`. A precise explanation of these input signatures and their relationship would resolve this confusion.

**Limitations:**
1.  The paper, while building on established concepts, is not entirely self-contained, particularly for readers unfamiliar with the foundational work on PAC prediction sets, such as reference [3] (Park et al., 2020). Concepts like the exact definition of an `\epsilon`-correct parameter `\tau` (Definition 1) and the `(\epsilon, \delta)`-PAC property (Definition 2) are central to understanding the meta-PAC extension, but are introduced quite concisely. I would strongly suggest dedicating a few more paragraphs in Section 3.1 or an expanded background section to fully explain these core concepts from [3], their intuition, and how `\tau` is typically estimated, to make the paper more accessible to a broader machine learning audience.
2.  The experimental evaluation, while broad in terms of datasets, relies exclusively on the prototypical network [11] as the underlying meta-model for the score function. While prototypical networks are a strong baseline, the general applicability of Meta-PS, as claimed, would be further strengthened by demonstrating its performance with other prominent meta-learning algorithms, such as MAML [12] or optimization-based approaches. This would provide greater confidence in the method's robustness across different meta-learning paradigms.
3.  As highlighted previously in the weaknesses, the notation used throughout the paper remains a significant source of confusion. This applies particularly to the score function `f` and the various forms of the prediction set `F_tau`. For example, `F_tau(x)` in Definition 1, `F_tau(A, X)` in Figure 1, and `F_tau(A, x, y)` in Section 4.1. A more consistent and clearly defined notational scheme, perhaps with a dedicated notation table, would dramatically improve readability and understanding.

**Rating:**
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations.
